Pete Blackshaw, CEO & Co-Founder, BrandRank.AI  ·  The Future Of… Conference  ·  Chicago, April 12, 2026

Speech Synthesis

Pete Blackshaw argues that the AI revolution is fundamentally a consumer expectations revolution — and that brands are failing to keep pace. With 2.5 billion AI prompts fired daily, consumers now expect answers that are elegant, empathetic, and immediate. Yet most brand websites remain cluttered, conversion-obsessed, and structurally incapable of answering basic questions. Blackshaw calls this the “Answer Divide” and frames it as the defining design challenge of our time. His call to action: the design community must claim ownership of the answer experience, optimize for curiosity as fiercely as for conversion, and build brand knowledge systems that can survive AI’s most powerful truth detectors. The brands that win the Answer Economy won’t necessarily have the biggest AI budgets — they’ll be the ones with the courage to simply answer the question.

1

Opening: A Little Design Credibility

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Thank you for having me. I want to use this time to push on something I care deeply about — and to frame it as a genuine call to action. Today is about designing for answers.

I should establish upfront: I am not a designer by training. But I do have a small amount of design credibility. I made my way through college partly by designing the logo for UC Santa Cruz’s iconic mascot — the Banana Slug. The challenge was taking something grotesque and turning it into something people would love. Hence the glasses, the attitude, and eventually a hundred variations. It ended up being immortalized by Quentin Tarantino — it appears in Pulp Fiction — so I’ll take that as a win.

2

Context: Consumer, Digital, and Trust

I’ve always been passionate about three things: digital, the consumer, and trust. That thread runs from Procter & Gamble to Nestlé to Nielsen, through consumer trust startups, and through my first book on consumer empowerment. My new book, The Answer Economy, drops in September and is already on Amazon. The core argument is simple: the brands that win will be the ones that answer the question. AI magnifies the stakes enormously — but the underlying principle is timeless.

At BrandRank.AI, we work with roughly eighty of the world’s top brands. We use the LLMs themselves to measure brand health and trust — think of us as the world’s most rigorous AI auditor. We run brand claims through every major answer engine and surface what’s really being said, tracking across three dimensions: visibility (do you show up?), vulnerability (how do you show up — and are you exposed to misinformation or hallucination?), and content readiness (is your site structured to feed the algorithms?). The number-one predictor of answer share in the LLMs is your brand website content. Build a strong knowledge base, close the answer gap, and you will show up better, more accurately, and more consistently across every AI platform.

3

Today’s Challenge: Brands Fail at the Most Human Thing

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

We keep talking about human-centered AI — I’ve heard the phrase at least ten times in the last hour. But thirty years into the digital era, with billions spent on UX, we became extraordinarily good at guiding consumer behavior — and embarrassingly bad at actually answering consumer questions.

The problem isn’t lack of content. It’s lack of organizational ownership. Who owns answers inside a typical company? Marketing owns messaging. Legal owns risk. R&D owns claims. Corporate Affairs owns reputation. Digital owns SEO. Nobody owns the answer. And where nobody owns it, nobody fixes it. My argument is that design should own this — because design is closest to empathy, closest to simplicity, and closest to how consumers actually feel. Every management team I speak with is confused about AI governance. That confusion is your opportunity.

“Design has never had a bigger leadership moment than right now.”

4

Market Reality: From Impressions to Answers

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Let me ground this in the numbers. 2.5 billion AI prompts are fired every day — and that’s likely an undercount, because Google itself has effectively become an answer engine. The top of nearly every search results page is now AI-generated.

We have moved from the old world of ads, search, and clicks to a new world of question, synthesis, and decision — what I call the Prompted Moment of Truth. The consumer is looking for an answer, whether on your website or from Claude, and what shows up defines the relationship. In Search 1.0, you competed for ten blue links. Now you compete for one integrated response.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Our consumer research confirms this shift across every category. Even low-involvement categories are implicated. But if you’re in health, in nutrition, in personal care — consumers are going to AI for answers at an extraordinary rate. The question is whether your brand is showing up with authority, or getting bypassed entirely.

AI is first and foremost a consumer expectations revolution, not a technology revolution. When 2.5 billion people are prompting daily and getting responses that are elegant, empathetic, personalized, and ad-free, their tolerance for brand experiences that are static, uninviting, and salesy collapses very quickly. And brands are not prepared.

5

The Answer Divide

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

LLMs are elegant, empathetic, personalized, conversational, ad-free, and multisensory — and they improve every single day. Brand answer experiences have flatlined. In many cases they’ve gotten worse, because e-commerce teams have taken over brand websites and optimized entirely for conversion, with zero weight given to curiosity, education, or trust-building.

I’ve framed this as the Answer Divide — a widening gap between LLM answer readiness, rising sharply over time, and brand answer readiness, which remains flat or declines. On the LLM side: 24/7, elegant, empathetic, personalized, ad-free. On the brand side: static, uninviting, incomplete, too salesy, high friction. The gap between those two lines is the opportunity in front of this room.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Here’s what typical brand website behavior looks like. You arrive at a beautifully designed homepage — the interface says “How can I help you?” — and before you can type a single character, you’re hit with a pop-up discount offer, an email capture, a countdown timer, a chat request, and a notification prompt, simultaneously. Someone in a C-suite approved every one of those interruptions, believing each one builds loyalty. The contrast on the left — a clean, open interface that simply invites a question — is an illustration of what’s possible when design is put in service of the consumer rather than the conversion funnel. This is precisely where the design community needs to intervene.

“When a consumer gets a better answer from a chatbot than from a product page — that’s a design failure.”

6

What’s at Stake: Answer Share Tracks Market Share

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Here’s what is getting the C-suite’s attention. The challenger brands — the smaller players with no big media budgets who spend everything on content — know how to answer every consumer question imaginable. They leave the big incumbent brands in the dust on answer share. And increasingly, answer share tracks market share. Challengers win by explaining, not shouting. That is a principle every person in this room can take back to their organization today.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Brands winning in the Answer Economy — Patagonia, IKEA, Sephora — aren’t necessarily winning because of an AI strategy. They’re winning because they decided to be transparent, curious-first, and answer-ready long before AI entered the conversation. Patagonia almost certainly has no formal AI strategy. But they are so fundamentally answer-ready — so transparent about materials, sourcing, employees, and values — that they’re winning answer share across virtually every relevant category. Don’t be obsessed with your AI strategy. Focus on your consumer strategy.

7

Brand Examples: The Gap in Practice

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Dove is fascinating. Ask their site “what are the ingredients of Dove?” and you receive 493 product results, zero editorial content. Zero. Compare that to a redesigned experience that responds with “You asked: What are the ingredients of Dove? We believe in transparency. Here’s what goes into our products.” That’s not a new content strategy. That’s a design decision — and it’s the kind of decision that builds trust with the consumers most likely to become your most loyal advocates.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close
⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Tide is another telling example. Tide runs sustainability messaging in nearly every earnings call and TV campaign. Yet searching “is Tide sustainable?” on their own website returns one result with no meaningful answer. The before-and-after here tells the whole story. The redesigned version — an illustration of what’s possible — surfaces six organized results with a quick answer immediately: “Yes. We use 100% renewable electricity in our owned operations and are reducing water usage by 30%.” The content exists. It just needs to be designed to surface. When consumers don’t find it on the brand site, they go to ChatGPT — and whatever ChatGPT says becomes the truth.

Left: current Tide.com search. Right: illustration of an answer-first redesign.
⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Colgate’s website functions more like a knowledge engine than a product catalog — surfacing answers on cavities, ingredients, and oral health science the moment you type. It’s a simple but powerful design choice: treat every search query as a consumer asking a real question, and answer it. That approach is paying off meaningfully in AI answer share.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

I’ve done cluster analysis on almost all of your brands, and there’s a fascinating spectrum between optimizing for curiosity and optimizing for conversion. The vast majority of brands have moved firmly toward conversion in the last few years. But the outliers are telling — purpose-driven brands like Seventh Generation and Ben & Jerry’s way over-index on answering questions, and they’re being rewarded in AI answer share. Curiosity and conversion are not at odds. Curiosity is the doorway to conversion. We just need design principles that build in patience before the pitch.

“AI doesn’t care about org charts — but it exposes them.”

8

Action Steps for the Design Community

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

The first step is getting back to basics. When P&G built its first interactive marketing team in the late 1990s, we almost instinctively operated in a dialogue mode — asking consumers questions, answering theirs, creating genuine exchange. That spirit got lost somewhere in the transition to “digital.” The assignment now is to bring it back. Build websites that sell by serving. Build Q&A libraries that educate. Create content that works for humans and machines simultaneously.

The second step is optimizing for curiosity first. Most brand websites are built on the assumption that every visitor is ready to buy. They’re not. Many are researching, comparing, or worrying. Design principles must balance for patience. Convert existing pages into answers — the content is largely already there, it just needs to be redesigned to surface it.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

The third step is mastering the Ask-Through Rate. Everyone is debating clicks versus no-clicks in an AI world. That’s the wrong debate. The real metric is the sequential journey of prompt, answer, interrogation, and validation. Amazon’s Rufus is the best current model: ask a question, get a clarification, receive an adapted answer, address adjacent concerns, validate understanding, and arrive at a product recommendation. The Amazon CEO has reported that 60% of consumers who engage with one question on Rufus purchase more product. That is curiosity unlocking conversion at scale — and a design principle every brand can learn from.

The fourth step is nailing the first impression. Think carefully about the persona your brand projects the moment a consumer arrives. Is it the warm, knowledgeable concierge who says “Of course, allow me, here’s your answer”? Or the overwhelmed bureaucrat stamping “Insufficient Information”? The concierge persona generates first-party data, deepens the relationship, and earns the right to make a product suggestion.

The fifth step is designing for Claude — the BS detector. If a brand claim can’t survive Claude’s scrutiny, it is spin rather than substance. Brands that can document and substantiate their claims are the ones winning in AI answer environments.

9

The Answer Economy Design Oath

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

I want to close with something I’d like you to carry out of this room. The design community needs its own oath for this moment.

Take the oath — right now
◆

We will use design to make things understood, not just seen.

◆

We will treat every question as a signal of intent, not friction.

◆

We will design for curiosity as fiercely as we design for conversion.

◆

We will build knowledge, not just interfaces.

◆

We will have the courage to answer the questions brands would rather avoid.

⛶ tap to enlarge
✕ tap anywhere to close

Grab this. It is your moment. And I’ll leave you with one final truth worth a thousand strategy decks: you don’t need an AI strategy. You need a consumer strategy. Anticipate your consumers’ needs and questions — AI-friendly content will follow. Earn trust with real, proven value. Document your claims and stand behind them. Trust earns visibility. Visibility powers growth.

You don’t need an AI strategy.
You need a consumer strategy.

brandrank.ai  ·  The Answer Economy  ·  September 2026
